entry.title: Re: ABAP test cockpit (ATC) recommendations for governance of clean core ABAP development
entry.content:
Excellent and detailed post  the shift from the 3-tier extensibility model to the Clean Core Level Concept is exactly the kind of practical reframing that teams needed. The A–D level framework gives teams a structured classification for every extension and a consistent way to manage the associated upgrade risk  Level A for fully upgrade-safe ABAP Cloud extensions, Level B for classic but documented APIs with governance sign-off, Level C for internal objects on a documented remediation roadmap, and Level D for modifications that should carry a retirement timeline. That clarity was missing from the binary clean/not-clean model.
The point about ATC exemption logs is particularly important in practice  an exemption log that grows without regular review is one of the clearest early indicators of governance drift, and tracking exemption volume and approval rationale as a Clean Core KPI catches that trend before it becomes a landscape-level problem. Most teams treat exemptions as a one-way door and never revisit them.
The recommendation to use ATC on SAP BTP as the central check system connected across all development-related systems  with blocking mode active for Priority 1 and 2 findings on transport tasks is the right architecture for any team serious about governance at scale. The inclusion of SAP CVA security checks without separate licensing is also a meaningful practical benefit that often gets overlooked in the conversation.
One question worth raising: for organizations with large volumes of Level C and D code identified after the initial ATC readiness run, what's the recommended approach to sequencing the remediation roadmap  is the guidance to tackle the highest-frequency objects first, the highest-risk upgrade objects first, or to align remediation with the business process migration sequence in the S/4HANA project plan?

entry.title: Re: CLM - Contract and Lease Management Migration in Publ...
entry.content:
Good day @SonyaM 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the details you shared, the issue is not the migration cockpit itself. What you are experiencing is caused by the valuation input data of your specific contract. In your case, the condition, the condition with valuation property Present Value Specified is higher than the contract value, and this leads exactly to the negative interest calculation errors you listed, including RECEPR151 and the related valuation inconsistencies.

What is important in the case you described is, when you migrate a lease contract with one-time statistical condition NPV Default using Present Value Specified, the system does not use the interest rate your manually entered from the valuation parameters as the effective driver for the valuation. See KBA 3041660 for more details.

So, if the contract you migrated has Present Value Specified = 96,898.99 and Correct Value = 76,762.25, then the present value is already greater than the value of the future lease payments. See Notes 3531206  for more insights on that exact setup producing a negative calculated interest rate from inconsistent valuation input.

So, yes, the behaviour you experience is expected when Present Value Specified exceeds Contract Value. The system is not ignoring a valid setup. It is rejecting an inconsistent one.

You must correct the Present Value Specified so that it is equal to or lower than the contract value for the lease payments considered in valuation. In your example, 96,898.99 cannot be used together with a contract value of 76,762.25. I suggest you fix this first.

For your type of migrated contract, you should make the correction in the migrated valuation input itself, meaning in the statistical migration condition that carries Presen Value Specified, not try to force the valuation through the interest rate field. See KBA 3041660 for more details.

For this contract a practical migration approach would be for you to:

  1. Review the original source migration data for the Asset Default Value and Net Present Value Default conditions.
  2. Verify whether the NPV amount was loaded incorrectly, or whether the contract value basis is incomplete, for example missing or reduced lease cash flows.
  3. Correct the contract so that the Present Value Specified condition is no greater than the contract value.
  4. Re run valuation simulation only after that correction.

If your contract was already migrated incorrectly, then I would recommend the following references:

3409502 - Process to perform early termination on contracts which have inconsistent data due to incorrect initial migration - SAP for Me

2544232 - Leasing: Handling transition scenarios - SAP for Me

 

To conclude, my recommendation/the decision to consider is:

  • Either you adjust the Present Value Specified downward, or
  • You correct the underlying lease cash flows upward if the contract value is currently understated.      

What I would not advice is to rely on the manually entered interest rate to compensate for that mismatch, because that is not how the valuation logic works when present value is specified.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How to Avoid the Most Common Account Assignment Object issues in SAP Asset Accounting (FI-AA)
entry.content:
Working in SAP Asset Accounting (FIAA) often means dealing with recurring and sometimes puzzling error messages such as:

  • GLT2201
  • AAPO189 / AAPO190
  • FAA_POST042

Although these messages may appear unrelated, they all share a common root cause:

👉issues in the configuration or derivation of Additional Account Assignment Objects during FI-AA postings.

In this blog, you’ll learn why these errors occur, how to analyse them, and most importantly how to prevent them through proper configuration.

 

🧩 1. What Are Account Assignment Objects in Asset Accounting?

In SAP FI-AA, each asset can carry specific account assignment information used during postings. These objects fall into two main categories:

1.1 Time-Dependent Account Assignment Objects 

(stored in table ANLZ)

These include:

    • Internal order (CAUFN)

    • WBS element (Costs) (PS_PSP_PNR2)
    • Cost center (KOSTL)
    • Profit Center (PRCTR)
    • …

They are used exclusively during:

    • Depreciation postings

    • Gain/loss postings
    • Interest postings

📌 Important:  They are not used for APC (Acquisition and Production Cost) postings. Therefore, these objects must point to real cost elements, not statistical ones.

1.2 Account Assignment Objects for APC Postings

APC postings require statistical objects, such as:

    • Investment Order (ANLA-EAUFN)

    • WBS element (ANLA-PS_PSP_PNR)
    • Cost Center (ANLZ-KOSTL)
    • …

These objects depend on statistical cost elements (category 90).

📌 Important:   To allow this, you must ensure system message KI098 is set to off or warning in transaction OBA5.

 

⚙️2. Key Configuration Steps in Asset Accounting

2.1 Activate Account Assignment Objects

SPRO path: 

Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Integration with General Ledger → Additional Account Assignment Objects → Activate Account Assignment Objects

Note: If you activate the Identical flag, SAP enforces that the account assignment object posted in the G/L document must match the one existing in the asset master.

2.2 Specify Account Assignment Types

SPRO path:

Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Integration with General Ledger → Additional Account Assignment Objects → Activate Account Assignment Objects → Specify Account Assignment Type for Account Assignment Objects

Here you define which objects are active for:

  • APC values posting
  • Depreciation run
  • Relevant transaction types

📌 Best practice: Enable both APC and Depreciation settings for all transaction types.

2.3 Check if the validity of the Account Assignment Object

Ex: Cost Center (Transaction KS03): 

2.4 Account Assignment Object fields active in the Asset Master Data, to be derived.

Always ensure that the Account Assignment Object is set as Optional in the Asset Master Screen Layout

In case of PROFIT CENTER:

  • If Segment Reporting is active, you have Profit Center field in the Asset Master Data, Time-Dependent Tab.
    Profit Center must be set as Optional in the Asset Master Screen Layout
  • If Segment Reporting is not active, Profit Center can be derived from:
    •  Cost Center (ANLZ-KOSTL).
    •  WBS (ANLA-PS_PSP_PNR) or ORDER (ANLA-EAUFN)
    • Substitution
    • Default Account Assignment OKB9

       In these cases, points 2.1, 2.2 and 2.3 must be fulfilled and maintained in the Asset Master Data.

2.5 G/L Account configuration:

The correct functioning of account assignment derivation depends heavily on how G/L accounts and field controls are configured.

  1. Check whether the Asset Reconciliation accounts (B/S account) manage CO account assignments statistically:
    --> In S/4HANA system: Record Account Assignment flag in FS00 transaction (Control Data tab)


--> In ECC system: Account should have a Cost Element with Statistical Cost Element Category (90)

 

Note that for accounts of cost element category 11 "Revenues" or 12 "Sales Deductions", the objects from the asset master record are only assigned if revenue  posting is allowed in the corresponding CO object.

For instance, for the cost center to be derived it must not be blocked for revenues in transaction KS03:

 

2. Make sure that the account assignment objects are ready for input in the field status of the G/L account and the field status of the posting keys. Hidden objects are not copied to the line items. (Example: If you grey out the Cost Center in the field status, a Profit Center derivation cannot be performed for this line item).

 

🚨Understanding the Most Common FI-AA Errors

Here’s a clear explanation of why each error happens.

🔸GLT2201 — “Balancing field & in line item & not filled”

Cross-application error that is due to a wrong derivation of the Account Assignment objects that intervene in the posting of a FI document.

Occurs because in the "Document splitting" customizing, you have indicated the Profit Center as "Zero balance" and "Mandatory". However, you are still not deriving a Profit Center from the asset master in relation to asset line items posted in the FI-GL document.

SPRO: Financial Accounting--> General Ledger Accounting --> Document Splitting--> Define Document Splitting Characteristics for General Ledger Accounting occurs when:

 

🔸AAPO189 / AAPO190 — “Account object &1 differs from that in asset master record"

Occurs because an account assignment object that differs from the asset master record was entered in the document, because:

  • It is not derived from the Asset Master record
  • There is a substitution, exits or BTE that may impact the account assignment derivation
  • Different Account Assignment Object from an investment measure (WBS, order)

This check is done when the “identical” indicator is set as explained in point 2.1

🔸FAA_POST042 — “Balance in techn. clearing account: ledger &1, currency &2, &3, &4"

Occurs when posting an integrated business transaction (for instance, an integrated asset acquisition). The system splits the business transaction into an operational and a valuating document. Both documents are posted to account Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition, which has to display a balance of zero at the account assignment objects level, otherwise transaction cannot be posted. 

  • Ensure that the Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition (SPRO: Financial Accounting --> Asset Accounting--> Integration with General Ledger Accounting --> Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition) and the fixed asset balance sheet account have the same field control.  
  • The Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition must be able to manage CO account assignments statistically.
  • Ensure that no substitution, exits or BTE exist, that may impact the account assignment derivation.

 

🧠 5. Summary & Best Practices

To avoid these account assignment object errors:

✔ Activate relevant assignment objects in customizing

  • SPRO path: Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Integration with General Ledger → Additional Account Assignment Objects → Activate Account Assignment Objects
  • Transaction: ACSET

✔ Set the Account Assignment Objects as Optional in the different Layouts:

  • Asset Master Data Layout: SPRO path: Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Master Data →Screen Layout →Define Screen Layout for Asset Master Data
  • G/L Account Field Status Group: SPRO path → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Ledgers → Fields → Define Field Status Variant (OBC4) 
  • Posting Key Field Status: SPRO path→ Financial Accounting → General Ledger Accounting → Document Entry → Define Posting Keys (OB41)

 ✔ Maintain correct Account Assignment Objects in the Asset Master Data or alternative Object to be derived from.

✔ Check G/L accounts

  • Reconciliation accounts must allow statistical assignments
  • Ensure system message KI098 is set to off or warning in transaction OBA5

✔ Validate Account Assignment Objects

  • Cost Centers, Orders, WBS elements, etc, must be valid and active.

By ensuring these configuration elements are aligned across FI, CO, and AA, you can prevent the majority of FI-AA posting issues and maintain smooth asset-related financial processes.

entry.title: Strengthen Kafka integration
entry.content:
Hello community!

For an international customer, I’m looking for insights to strengthen the integration with Kafka, in an EDA context.

I’m specifically looking for these topics

  • Private Link Architecture. B2B VPN tunnel, Cloud Connector, … ?
  • Multiple event types on a single topic
  • Better visibility on errors, especially when multiple schema rules are violated
  • Offset commit mechanism
  • AVRO schema support
  • Retry behavior and flexible strategy

Yes, I know there is Advanced Event Mesh. But the choice for Kafka was made a long time ago. Currently, we do not use the native Kafka adapter within the Integration Suite.

I’m thus looking for insights within the Integration Suite context to strengthen our current integration. To make it more robust, safe and futureproof.

Looking forward to your feedback!

Thanks.
Dimitri

entry.title: Centralized Error Handling in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Cloud Integration, runtime errors can occur due to various reasons such as script failures, adapter issues, or unexpected payloads. Handling such errors individually in every integration flow often leads to duplicated logic and increased maintenance effort.

This blog demonstrates a centralized error handling mechanism in SAP Cloud Integration that:

  • Captures errors using an Exception Subprocess
  • Forwards error data using the ProcessDirect adapter
  • Sends HTML-formatted email notifications
  • Is reusable across multiple business integration flows

Solution

The solution consists of two types of integration flows:

1. Business Integration Flows

  • Executes business logic
  • Uses an Exception Subprocess to capture errors
  • Sends error details to a centralized logger

2. Central Error Logger Integration Flows

  • Receives error messages from multiple iFlows
  • Normalizes error data
  • Sends styled HTML email notifications to the support team

Step 1: Create the Business Integration Flow

1. Navigate to the SAP Integration Suite

2. Go to Design → Integrations and APIs

3. Create a new package (example: PCK_CentralErrorLogging)

4. Create a new Integration Flow named Business_Test_Iflow

This iFlow represents a sample business process where a runtime error occurs.

 

Step 2: Configure the Sender and Main Process

1. Add an HTTPS Sender Adapter

  • Configure endpoint address (example: /testError)
  • Configure authentication as required

2. Connect the adapter to the Start Event

3. Add a Groovy Script named Script_Groovy_Generate_Error

  • This script deliberately throws an exception for testing purposes

4. Connect the script to the End Event

 

Step 3: Add Exception Subprocess for Error Capture

1. Add an Exception Subprocess to the Business iFlow

2. Add a Groovy Script inside the subprocess named Script_Groovy_CaptureError

The Groovy script performs the following responsibilities:

  • Capture integration flow name
  • Capture message ID
  • Generate correlation ID
  • Capture error message
  • Generate timestamps (IST and UTC)

All values are stored as message properties. Using an Exception Subprocess enables centralized error capture without modifying individual processing steps.

 

Step 4: Send Error Details via ProcessDirect Adapter

1. After the Groovy script, add a ProcessDirect Receiver Adapter

2. Configure the address as /centralErrorLogger

This enables reusability across multiple business iFlows.

Step 5: Create the Central Error Logger Integration Flow

1. Create an integration flow named Central_Error_Logger_Iflow

2. Configure the Sender Adapter as ProcessDirect

3. Use the same address /centralErrorLogger

Step 6: Normalize Incoming Error Data

1. Add a Groovy Script as the first step

2. The script reads error details from properties, headers, or payload and applies fallback values if data is missing, preparing a consistent structure for email formatting.

This makes the central logger reusable.

Step 7: Format Error Details into HTML

1. Add a Content Modifier

2. Create an HTML table displaying:

  • Integration Flow Name
  • Message ID
  • Adapter Type
  • Error Message
  • Timestamp (IST and UTC)
  • Correlation ID

 

Step 8: Configure Email Notification

1. Add a Mail Receiver Adapter

2. Configure mail server, sender and receiver email IDs, subject, and set MIME type to text/html

Step 9: End-to-End Testing

1. Deploy both integration flows

2. Trigger the business iFlow using the HTTP endpoint

3. Verify the following:

  • Business iFlow succeeds in Message Monitor
  • Central Error Logger receives the error
  • HTML email is delivered with error details

 

Validation Using Another Business Integration Flow

The same centralized error handling was validated with another real-world integration flow named Invoice_Batch_Email_Processor_With_ProcessDirect.

  • Multiple processing steps
  • Routers based on message severity
  • Multiple email notifications
  • Groovy scripts and content modifiers

 

Key Observations

  • Exception Subprocess captured runtime errors successfully
  • The same central error logger iFlow was reused
  • No changes were required in the logger
  • HTML email alerts were generated correctly

 

This confirms the solution works for both simple and complex integration flows.

Benefits of This Approach

  • Centralized error handling
  • Reusable across multiple iFlows
  • Clean separation of business logic and error handling
  • Faster troubleshooting using Message ID and Correlation ID
  • Professional HTML email notifications
  • Proven in real-world scenarios

Conclusion

By implementing centralized error handling using Exception Subprocess and ProcessDirect adapters, SAP Cloud Integration landscapes become easier to maintain, monitor, and support. This approach reduces duplication, improves observability, and scales efficiently across integration designs.

This solution was implemented and tested in SAP Integration Suite, and all screenshots are from real execution to ensure accuracy and practical relevance.

entry.title: Re: Cross-Plant PPDS Planning: Special Procurement Not Tr...
entry.content:
To achieve your scenario, maintain a source list entry in Plant A pointing to Plant B, ensure transportation lane exists, and confirm PDS is active in Plant B. This is the standard fit‑to‑standard approach for cross‑plant PP/DS planning in S/4HANA.

Would you like me to outline a step‑by‑step configuration checklist (special procurement, source list, lane, PDS) so you can validate each piece systematically? That usually helps pinpoint why MRP Live skipped the STR creation.

entry.title: Re: Error When Confirming Transfer Order – “Enter a Stora...
entry.content: That error message “Enter a storage unit” usually points to the fact that the warehouse you are working in is storage unit–managed (SU‑managed). In SU‑managed warehouses, every transfer order confirmation requires you to specify the storage unit number, because the system tracks stock at the storage unit level rather than just at bin level. Start by checking whether your warehouse is SU‑managed. If yes, you’ll need to enter or generate storage unit numbers consistently during TO creation and confirmation. If not, review the movement type and warehouse settings to remove the SU requirement.

entry.title: Re: How Contractual delivery date for ADHOC line gets determined in sales order in SAP
entry.content: Instead of using Subject Text for your requirement, use the box where you have to explain clearly your requirement with related screen shots. Make use of the Subject title effectively and avoid lengthy text

entry.title: Re: Tabela de vínculo de número de série em esboço (Draft...
entry.content: As far as I know, and based on the SAP Business One database structure, the serial number assignment in draft documents is handled via table ODSR. It links the draft document (DocEntry) and its item lines (BaseLinNum) with the corresponding serial numbers (SysSerial).

entry.title: Re: Partial payments/invoice for 1 purchased pc
entry.content: For negotiated milestone payments (e.g., 30% on PO issue, 30% on engineering approval, 40% on receipt), SAP supports this via down payment requests or milestone billing plans on the PO. These payments are not tied to GR. Instead, they are controlled by the payment terms and billing plan defined in the PO. Finance can process these payments against the PO using the agreed billing plan, with clear audit trail that it is a contractual milestone, not a goods receipt.

entry.title: Re: PPDS Process Order Not Visible in Fiori Confirmation...
entry.content: This is expected behavior, as Fiori confirmation apps use operation/phase scheduled dates rather than header dates. The scheduling result can be analyzed in /SAPAPO/RRP3; possible influencing factors include capacity constraints, resource sequencing/setup, or material availability, depending on the PP/DS planning situation. After confirmation, detailed scheduling logs are generally not retained, so identifying the exact reason typically requires reproducing the scenario with scheduling trace/log enabled.

entry.title: Re: Check on partner roles in PO
entry.content: In standard SAP, there is not a direct configuration switch that enforces “only allow invoicing parties from BP master data” when you change the PI partner in a PO. The system will let you assign any BP with the PI role, unless you add a control layer. Having said that, you cannot achieve this purely with customizing. The fit‑to‑standard approach is to use partner determination for proposal plus a validation enhancement (BAdI/custom logic) to enforce the restriction.

entry.content: excellent article.

entry.title: Absence counting config : Deduction quantity should include holidays on working days, not on weekoff
entry.content:
HI , 

This global client is only using absence module of Successfactors.

We have seen this common req. where they want deduction quantity to consider working days and holidays falling on working days. 

26th Mar - Working

27th Mar - Holiday + Working day

28thth Mar - Holiday + Non working day

29th Mar - Holiday + Non working day

29th Mar - Working day 

Leave applied from 26th to 29th Mar then it should  be 3 days . 

We tried using absence counting config. with holidays included as YES . 

But it reads 5 days then. 

If we use planned working time >=0 then as well the system takes it 0 for holiday falling on working day. 

We cant use weekdays - as they have dynamic workschedules with M-F working or THURSDAY to WED working. 

In absence counting rule as well we tried adding condition but could see any parameter that reads working days. 

Hence, as work around we are using PERIOD WORK SCHEDULE such that for days that have scheduled time we are marking them as SHIFT CLASSIFICATION as WORKING and excluding non working . 

That way in absence counting rule we are able to filter the condition if SHIFT = WORKING AND HOLIDAY = FULL then set deduction quantity = 1.

Please let us know if anyone has come across scenario through standard approach and how they achieved it.

entry.title: Re: How to have multiple schedules in service call whenev...
entry.content: According to standard SAP Business One behavior, multiple schedules should be supported when “Enable Scheduling Multiple Tickets” is active in Document Settings (Per Document → Service Call). If saving additional schedules fails when a contract is assigned, this is not expected as I know; please verify the configuration and check for add-ons or Transaction Notification logic. If reproducible in a standard scenario, please raise an SAP Business One support incident.

entry.title: What’s New in Product Change Management and why should I care?
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post where I will share the latest updates on Product Change Management in SAP Cloud ERP Private including an exciting sneak preview of our upcoming Change Management Agent.  Please note that Change Management Agent is a BETA version, any version that will be generally available in the future may work differently!

Nowadays, product change management requires speed, control, and compliance to operate in lockstep. Organizations need a clear, repeatable process to capture issues, assess impact, and implement changes across engineering, manufacturing, and quality—without adding risk or rework. That foundation depends on a centralized system of record, defined roles and workflows, transparent approvals, and strong auditability.

No time to read? For more details about the latest updates in change management, including FAQs, sign up and access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert session.

A modern change process starts with clear problem definition, followed by structured evaluation and disciplined execution. Each stage relies on consistent data capture, governance, and cross-functional visibility. The core artifact is a change record that links people, processes, and products. It assigns accountable teams, enforces workflow steps, captures digital signatures, and consolidates all affected objects—materials, BOMs, specs, recipes, and related documents. Mass assignment lets users add multiple materials and document records in a single action, cutting administrative work. Regulatory modes tighten controls where needed, blocking out-of-process edits and keeping every change on a defined path. This creates a compliant framework that scales across product lines and business units.

With the past releases, we added a number of new features to help your companies reach their goals: 

Enhancements to Change Objects:

 

  • Document info record, production routing, task lists and product variant structure as  supported objects for regulatory mode
  • Smart links enhancements
  • Custom objects, mass assignment of objects

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

Enhancements to Process Routes:

  • Add creator/responsible of CR as workflow recipient
  • Send email for process route activities (Approve, Reject, etc.)
  • Access attachments of workflow items in CR
  • CR embedded in My Inbox
  • Dynamic team determination

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

Usability is pivotal for adoption and data quality. Copy functions allow users to replicate a change record with only the needed sections—descriptions, items, documents—speeding creation without carrying noise forward. Rich text editing and resizable description panes promote complete, in-line documentation, improving downstream search and review. Default relevance types for change items remove repetitive selections while preserving exceptions. Workflow updates route tasks to creators or accountable owners, embed tasks in the inbox with full context, trigger email notifications, and require digital signatures at the task level. Traceability strengthens without slowing execution. These pragmatic improvements reduce cycle time and make governance feel embedded, not imposed.

Enhancements to Usability and Collaboration:

  • Copy change records
  • Enlarged detailed description with rich text editor
  • Default item relevance for change items

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

Enhancements to Digital Signature:

  • Digital signature as part of workflow

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

And what about AI? How can it help me perform my daily work more efficiently?

AI extends this rigor by removing friction from high-frequency tasks and surfacing insight at the moment of decision. Traditional search and review across change records, documents, routings, and BOMs is slow and fragmented, especially across multiple record types and regions, which drives duplication, missed context, and delays. Conversational assistance for ad hoc queries and summarization enables engineers and reviewers to locate, understand, and act on change data in minutes rather than hours.

By targeting search, filtering, traceability, and quick summaries first, teams gain immediate productivity without reengineering the process landscape. The result is faster cycles with proven compliance, supported by a system that maintains control while accelerating decision-making.

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

Can we go a few steps further?

Already today, targeted AI amplifies productivity gains by removing bottlenecks. Our Change Record Management Agent supports traceability, audit compliance, and greater efficiency in managing product and design changes through where-used analysis, change impact summarization, similarity search and agent-guided creation of new change records.

  • Improves change coordinator’s productivity
  • Reduces engineering change cost
  • Reduces cost of new product introduction to market.

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

On the way: AI-Assisted Similarity Check, Change Record Creation, and Summarization

We won’t stop there: Soon, our conversational assistant will help you cut rework and improve decisions. Run similarity checks across all change items, get intelligent proposals for change creation, and workflows. Done well and with the help of AI, change management becomes a strategic capability—compliant by design, collaborative by default, and continuously optimized by data—so teams find the right records fast, grasp context in seconds, and initiate well‑formed changes with less effort.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

For more details about the latest updates in change management, including FAQs, sign up and access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert session.

More Information / Additional Links 

Also, check out our What’s New document for regular updates!

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and refer to our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: Liberating SAP agents with the Solace Agent Mesh
entry.content:
• Liberating SAP agents with the Solace Agent Mesh
  • Introduction
  • The Agentic AI Landscape: From LLMs to Enterprise Orchestration
  • AI Foundation Models & Hyperscalers
  • Agentic Development Frameworks
  • Integration & Orchestration Platforms
  • Enterprise Data & Business Systems
  • The Structural Problem with Agentic AI in SAP Environments
  • The Incoming RFQ Agent: Complexity Hidden in Plain Sight
  • The Solace Agent Mesh
  • What Is Solace Agent Mesh?
  • Architecture Overview
  • Core Components
  • Why SAP customers should use Solace Agent Mesh to build their agentic platform
  • The Multi-Platform Reality of Enterprise AI
  • How Solace Agent Mesh Addresses the Multi-Platform Challenge
  • Key Takeaways
  • Appendix and links
  • Next Steps

Liberating SAP agents with the Solace Agent Mesh

Introduction

SAP landscapes hold decades of transactional history, business rules, and process intelligence across S/4HANA, ECC, BTP, and connected systems.

As organizations deploy AI agents on this data, five questions keep surfacing:

  • How do you build agents that scale beyond prototypes?
  • How do you connect agents across different platforms?
  • How do you enforce governance and auditability?
  • How do you validate each decision an agent makes?
  • How do you avoid rebuilding when the tech stack changes?

These questions explain why enterprise AI initiatives on SAP and other platforms stall. Most agentic frameworks excel at prototyping but weren't built for SAP's compliance, reliability, and integration requirements. They answer, "can we build something intelligent?" but not "can we run it in production with confidence?" That gap matters.

Solace Agent Mesh closes that gap. For organizations running SAP BTP Integration Suite with Advanced Event Mesh (built on Solace Event Broker), the architecture will look familiar, Solace Agent Mesh extends patterns you already use.

The Agentic AI Landscape: From LLMs to Enterprise Orchestration

Building production-grade agentic AI for SAP environments requires understanding the core technology landscape. The ecosystem spans four essential layers that autonomous systems require.

AI Foundation Models & Hyperscalers

At the top of the stack sit the large language models that power agent reasoning. AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, and Google Vertex AI provide managed access to models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, and others.

Key consideration for SAP customers: the choice of foundation model often depends on existing cloud commitments and compliance requirements particularly for European customers subject to GDPR who may prefer EU-hosted models.

Agentic Development Frameworks

This layer provides the scaffolding for building multi-agent systems. LangChain and LangGraph dominate the Python ecosystem, while CrewAI and AutoGen focus on multi-agent collaboration patterns. Microsoft's Semantic Kernel serves C# and .NET environments common in enterprise Microsoft shops. LlamaIndex specializes in data retrieval and indexing patterns.

The key limitation: These frameworks excel at agent logic but lack native enterprise integration capabilities. They assume you'll handle connectivity to SAP, security, and event-driven orchestration separately.

Integration & Orchestration Platforms

Tools like n8n, Zapier, and Apache Airflow bridge the gap between agents and business systems. They provide visual workflow builders and pre-built connectors useful for simple integrations but problematic at scale.

The challenge for SAP customers: these platforms typically use polling mechanisms rather than event-driven architectures, introducing latency and inefficiency. A workflow that "checks SAP every 5 minutes" doesn't meet real-time business requirements and places an unnecessary workload on SAP backends.

Enterprise Data & Business Systems

This is where your business logic and data live. SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, Salesforce, and other systems of record hold the information agents need to make decisions. The expanded SAP ecosystem (Ariba, SuccessFactors, Concur) adds complexity; each system has unique APIs, authentication models, and data schemas.

The problem: Most agentic frameworks treat these as external APIs to be called via HTTPS.

The Structural Problem with Agentic AI in SAP Environments

SAP landscapes are distributed by design, on-premises or cloud ECC or S/4HANA, cloud extensions on BTP, third-party CRM and logistics, plus SaaS tools. Making AI agents work across this estate isn't an AI problem. It's an integration problem.

Most generic agentic frameworks promise autonomous decision-making and multi-step workflows. But deploying them in SAP environments exposes a core disconnect between consumer-grade agentic systems and enterprise needs.

Let's examine these challenges through a common scenario: automating incoming RFQ response workflows.

The Incoming RFQ Agent: Complexity Hidden in Plain Sight

Your sales team receives 200+ RFQs daily via email, supplier portals, and EDI. They want an agent to automate responses:

"When an RFQ arrives, extract requirements, check product information and cross/up selling opportunities in PIM, check inventory availability in S/4HANA, calculate pricing with margin rules, calculate shipping costs and delivery dates based on sourcing warehouse and destination and send it back all within 1 hour."

This seems straightforward until you map it to your SAP landscape and discover why most enterprise agentic AI projects fail.

Challenge 1: Foundation Models Lack SAP Domain Knowledge

Your agent must interpret unstructured RFQ text ("need 500 units of product SKU : SKF-6206-2Z, ship to Munich warehouse by May 15") and map it to: material master data in S/4HANA, customer-specific pricing conditions, product attributes in PIM, and warehouse locations for shipping calculations. When the RFQ mentions " SKF-6206-2Z," the LLM doesn't know this maps to material 100234567 with specific pricing procedure ZSTD and related cross-sell items.

The Gap: RAG systems retrieve documents. Your agent needs real-time structured data from SAP tables (MARA, MARC, A304), PIM hierarchies, and customer-specific contracts with relationships intact and authorization context preserved.

Challenge 2: Frameworks Can't Orchestrate SAP Transactional Workflows

Your workflow requires the following sequential process:

Steps 8-9 are transactional. If quote generation fails after inventory reservation, you must roll back. If credit is blocked at step 4, abort before reserving stock. Your agent needs distributed transaction support, compensation logic, and state persistence (processing may span systems with different response times).

The Gap: LangChain and CrewAI lack long-running workflow state management and intelligent retry mechanisms. While the RAG agent querying a PIM system responds in 5 seconds, the SAP Business Agent Foundation (prototype) agent querying a S/4HANA system might need multiple minutes. LangChain and CrewAI can't maintain context across these time spans, they timeout, lose state, or retry incorrectly.

Challenge 3: Integration Platforms Create Latency and Data Loss

Using Zapier or n8n means polling-based triggers (5–15-minute delays), HTTP-only connectivity (no RFC, IDoc, or BAPI), and lost business context (customer sales area, pricing group, plant assignment).

Real Failure: RFQ arrives at 9:03 AM, detected at 9:15 AM. Agent queries inventory via REST API but doesn't see stock reserved at 9:08 AM by another process. Your agent commits to delivery you can't fulfill. One hour later: "Sorry, inventory is unavailable."  and customer trust is damaged.

The Gap: You need event-driven triggers (millisecond reaction), protocol bridging (email → RFC → REST → EDI), semantic routing (route by customer region), and backpressure handling across systems with different performance profiles.

Challenge 4: Governance and Auditability Are Non-Negotiable

Your agent makes business decisions: pricing, inventory allocation, shipping commitments. SAP requires authorization context: a sales rep approves quotes up to €50K with 10% discount maximum; managers handle up to €500K with 20% discounts. It requires approval workflows, audit trails capturing every decision's reasoning, and compliance controls blocking sanctioned customers.

The Gap: Generic frameworks use technical service accounts bypassing SAP authority checks. LLM reasoning isn't logged in SAP. When auditors ask, "Why did we offer 12% discount and premium shipping?", the answer exists only in framework logs and not in SAP's audit trail.

These challenges block agentic POCs from reaching production. You need infrastructure designed for this problem.

The Solace Agent Mesh

What Is Solace Agent Mesh?

Solace Agent Mesh is a purpose-built orchestration platform that bridges the gap between agentic AI frameworks and production enterprise systems. It provides the event-driven infrastructure, protocol translation, state management, and governance capabilities required to deploy autonomous AI agents at scale in hybrid enterprise environments.

Architecture Overview

Solace Agent Mesh sits between three layers:

  • Input Layer: Event ingestion from enterprise systems, APIs, and user interfaces
  • Processing Layer: Agent orchestration, state management, and workflow control
  • Output Layer: Communication with systems, databases, services, and human approvers

Let's examine each core component that enables this architecture.

Core Components

The Solace Event Mesh

The foundation of Solace Agent Mesh is the enterprise grade SAP BTP Advanced Event Mesh that acts as the communication channel between all other components in the agentic platform.

The broker handles multiple protocols, connects to enterprise systems via built-in connectors, guarantees event delivery with dynamic routing, and processes high-throughput streams with sub-second latency.

Orchestration Engine

Solace Agent Mesh's orchestration engine manages complex, multi-step agentic workflows:

  • Long-running workflows: State persistence across hours or days for asynchronous processes
  • Checkpoint and resume: Automatic recovery from failures without workflow restart
  • Parallel execution: Concurrent agent invocation with result aggregation
  • Conditional routing: Dynamic workflow paths based on agent decisions or business rules
  • Human-in-the-loop: Seamless escalation to approval workflows when confidence thresholds aren't met

Agent Integration Framework

The Solace Agent Mesh framework can integrate with any Agent-to-Agent (A2A) compliant agent, Model Context Protocol (MCP) server or LLM provider

  • Multi-LLM support: Connect to 100+ models across AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, Google Vertex AI, Anthropic, OpenAI
  • Dynamic agent selection: Route tasks to optimal agents based on required capabilities
  • Agent registry: A growing catalog of Solace built, and community provided agents with capability declarations and access policies
  • Workflows: Build pro-code/low-code prescriptive workflows which will help build deterministic agentic solutions.

Agents remain stateless, but Solace Agent Mesh manages context, history, and coordination.

Data Management & Context Layer

Agents require real-time access to enterprise data and historical context:

  • Context enrichment: Automatic injection of relevant business context (customer history, product data, pricing rules)
  • Caching layer: Intelligent caching of frequently accessed data to reduce latency and LLM token costs
  • Context management: Results of individual agents are stored as artifacts which are then accessible to other required agents in the same session. This helps in cleaning up the overall context and avoiding context poisoning.

Data access respects authorization policies; agents inherit user permissions.

Governance & Observability

Production agentic systems require comprehensive governance:

  • Explainability: Capture and store LLM reasoning chains for regulatory compliance
  • Authorization & RBAC: Fine-grained access control with users, roles and groups
  • Real-time task visualization: An inbuilt task visualizer showing agent activity, success rates, latency, error patterns, token utilization in real time during the execution of the task.

Every agent’s action is traceable to a human user or automated trigger for full accountability.

Deployment Flexibility

Solace Agent Mesh supports hybrid and multi-cloud deployments:

  • On premises: Run Solace Agent Mesh in your data center alongside legacy systems
  • Cloud-native: Deploy on AWS, Azure, or GCP with Kubernetes orchestration
  • Container-based: Kubernetes for portable, scalable infrastructure

Solace Agent Mesh adapts to your infrastructure; you don't adapt to Solace Agent Mesh.

Why SAP customers should use Solace Agent Mesh to build their agentic platform

Let's return to the RFQ automation scenario. You've seen the four fundamental challenges:

  • Foundation models that don't understand SAP context
  • Frameworks that can't handle transactional workflows
  • Integration platforms that introduce latency
  • Complete absence of enterprise governance

Now consider what happens when you try to solve these problems with multiple disconnected solutions.

The Multi-Platform Reality of Enterprise AI

Most SAP customers build across platforms:

  • Customer service agents in Salesforce Agentforce
  • Product information using custom-built RAG agent querying PIM
  • Supply chain optimization using SAP Business Agent Foundation(prototype)
  • Shipping agent integrating with logistics and 3PL partners

Each of these was built to solve a specific departmental problem. Each uses different frameworks, different LLMs, and different integration patterns. And now you need them to work together.

The challenge: Your RFQ agent needs to coordinate across multiple platforms:

  • Query the Salesforce-based customer service agent for customer history and preferences
  • Query the RAG agent for getting product information, up/cross selling opportunities
  • Call the SAP Business Agent Foundation(prototype) agent for ATP, pricing and sourcing options
  • Trigger the logistics agent (third-party) for shipping calculation
  • Update the CRM agent with the final quote

Without unified orchestration, you build point-to-point integrations between platforms. The complexity explodes: 5 platforms need 20 integrations, 10 platforms need 90, each with its own auth, error handling, retries, and monitoring.

How Solace Agent Mesh Addresses the Multi-Platform Challenge

Solace Agent Mesh was designed specifically for this heterogeneous enterprise reality. Here's how it solves the RFQ automation scenario and enables cross-platform agent coordination:

 

 

Event-Driven Agent Coordination Across Platforms

The Problem Solved: Your RFQ arrives via email. Without Solace Agent Mesh, you'd build separate integrations to trigger agents on Salesforce, AWS, Azure, and SAP BTP, each with different authentication mechanisms and trigger patterns.

With Solace Agent Mesh:

An RFQ email triggers a workflow via SAP BTP Advanced Event Mesh. The orchestrator builds an execution plan, invokes the required agents, collates their responses, and returns a complete quote.

Result: One event, multiple interactions across platforms and no custom integration code required. When the customer service agent updates the customer context, it publishes back to the main orchestrator, making that data immediately available to all other agents in the workflow.

Unified State Management Across Agent Platforms

The Problem Solved: Your workflow spans 15-20 minutes across multiple agents: Salesforce responds in 20 seconds, PIM in 30 seconds, SAP in 4 minutes (querying warehouses), and shipping in 1 minute. Sequential execution plus wait states adds up.

With Solace Agent Mesh:

  • Workflow state persists in Solace Agent Mesh's orchestration engine, not in individual agent frameworks
  • If the SAP agent takes 10 minutes, Solace Agent Mesh maintains context and resumes exactly where it left off
  • Agents remain stateless; Solace Agent Mesh manages the correlation between RFQ ID, customer context, inventory results, pricing calculations, etc.

Result: A single workflow spanning Salesforce, SAP and custom agents maintain perfect state consistency without requiring any agent platform to handle long-running state.

Protocol Translation for Heterogeneous Agent Communication

The Problem Solved: Your Salesforce and RAG agents use A2A; SAP and shipping use REST APIs. You'd normally build protocol adapters for each.

With Solace Agent Mesh:

Proxy agents handle translation automatically. A2A agents connect via A2A proxy, REST agents via REST proxy. Solace Agent Mesh converts everything to events internally; you write zero adapter code.

Result: Zero custom protocol adapters. Each agent uses its native communication pattern; Solace Agent Mesh handles all translations.

Unified Governance and Audit Trail

The Problem Solved: Your RFQ agent made a decision: "Offer 12% discount, commit to May 15 delivery from Munich warehouse, total quote €47,350." That decision involved 3 different agents across 4 platforms. When your auditor asks, "Why this decision?", you have partial logs in Salesforce, SAP, and your custom agents - but no unified view.

With Solace Agent Mesh:

  • Every agent invocation, decision, and data exchange flow through Solace Agent Mesh's underlying Advanced Event Mesh Broker
  • Solace Agent Mesh's governance layer captures which user triggered the workflow, which agents ran (with inputs and sequence), and each LLM's reasoning chain
  • Final decision with full lineage: "12% discount approved because customer is Tier 1 (Salesforce), margin allows 15% (SAP pricing agent), historical win rate 78% at this discount level (analytics agent)"
  • Complete audit trail is accessible from Solace Agent Mesh's observability dashboard AND written to SAP Application Log (for SAP-centric teams)

Result: One source of truth for compliance, debugging, and process optimization, regardless of which platform hosted by which agent.

Future-Proof Agent Platform Flexibility

The Problem Solved: You use LangChain today. Next year you want to try n8n. When SAP releases its own framework, you'll want that too. Normally, each switch means rewriting integration.

With Solace Agent Mesh:

  • Agents interact with Solace Agent Mesh via standard protocols, their Solace Agent Mesh provided proxies and the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) specification
  • Switching from LangChain to n8n means updating the agent implementation, not the integration layer
  • Solace Agent Mesh's Agent Registry allows gradual migration: run LangChain agents alongside n8n agents, both participating in the same workflows
  • When SAP releases a new agentic framework, you add SAP agents to the mesh without touching existing other agents

Result: Avoid vendor lock-in and framework churn. Your integration investment in Solace Agent Mesh remains constant even as agent frameworks evolve.

Key Takeaways

Your SAP landscape spans multiple platforms. Your AI agents will too. What matters isn't having agents on Salesforce, AWS, and SAP; it's whether they work together or sit in silos.

Without a platform like Solace Agent Mesh, enterprises face what is called the "Integration Tax":

  • 80% of agentic AI project effort goes to integration, not intelligence
  • Exponential complexity as you add platforms: 5 platforms = 20 integrations, 10 platforms = 90 integrations
  • Brittle point-to-point connections that break when any platform updates
  • No unified governance: audit trails scattered across CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, SAP logs
  • Lost business context at every platform boundary: customer sales area, pricing group, plant assignment evaporates when moving from AWS to SAP

Solace Agent Mesh provides the orchestration layer that SAP enterprises need: native SAP connectivity, multi-platform coordination, transactional integrity, and enterprise governance. It's built for the reality you're operating in today and not the simplified demo scenarios that generic frameworks assume.

For SAP customers, Solace Agent Mesh isn't another tool. It's what gets agentic AI from pilot to production.

Appendix and links

  • Solace Agent Mesh: https://solace.com/products/agent-mesh/
  • Introduction to SAP Business Agent Foundation: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/ai-business-agents-and-the-evolution-of-business-automation-join-the/ba-p/13614232

Next Steps

  • Star the Solace Agent Mesh Github Repository
  • Try out the open-source Solace Agent Mesh : Open-source Solace Agent Mesh
  • Contact us to schedule a demo: Schedule a Solace Agent Mesh Demo
  • Ask to run a Solace Agent Mesh pilot : Run a pilot

entry.title: Re: Cross-Plant PPDS Planning: Special Procurement Not Tr...
entry.content:
Step 1: Special Procurement Key
In Plant A, assign Special Procurement 97 (procure from Plant B) in the material master (MRP 2 view). Confirm that procurement type is F (external procurement). This tells MRP that supply should come from Plant B.

Step 2: Source List
In Plant A, create a source list entry for Material 123 with supplying plant = Plant B. Mark it as fixed source if you want PP/DS to always choose Plant B. Without a source list, PP/DS may not recognize Plant B as a valid source.

Step 3: Transportation Lane
In PP/DS, check that a transportation lane exists from Plant B → Plant A. Use the “Create from Special Procurement” function to generate it automatically. Ensure validity dates cover your planning horizon.

Step 4: PDS in Supplying Plant
In Plant B, make sure Material 123 has Procurement type E (in‑house production), A valid BOM and routing/master recipe and a PP/DS PDS generated (via CIF integration or in‑system generation). This allows PP/DS to create planned orders in Plant B.

Step 5: MRP Live Execution
Run MD01N for Plant A. Expected outcome is Stock Transfer Requisition (or STR → PR) created in Plant A. Requirement transferred to Plant B. PP/DS generates a planned order in Plant B.

Step 6: Validation
Check MD04 in Plant A → STR should appear. Check /SAPAPO/RRP3 or PP/DS product view in Plant B → planned order should appear. If not, review logs for missing source determination or invalid transportation lane.

Step 7: Common Pitfalls

  • Source list missing or not valid.
  • Transportation lane dates not valid.
  • PDS not generated in Plant B.
  • Special procurement maintained incorrectly (wrong key or missing).
  • MRP Live restrictions (some classic MRP behaviors differ).

Recommendation:
Follow this checklist in order. The most common root cause in your scenario is missing source list in Plant A or invalid transportation lane. Once those are corrected, MRP Live should generate the STR in Plant A and the planned order in Plant B.

entry.title: How to Get Started with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
entry.content:
Why SAP Sustainability Data Exchange Matters

Sustainability doesn’t stop at company boundaries — it extends across the entire supply chain. To truly reduce environmental impact, organizations must collaborate and exchange reliable data with their partners.

SAP Sustainability Data Exchange enables businesses to securely share product carbon footprint data, improving transparency and accelerating decarbonization efforts.

With SAP Sustainability Data Exchange, companies can:

  • Collaborate with partners on sustainability data
  • Increase transparency across the supply chain
  • Standardize and share product carbon footprint information
  • Support data-driven decarbonization initiatives

What You’ll Learn in This Learning Journey

This short, 1-hour 22-minute course introduces the key concepts and practical steps to start using SAP Sustainability Data Exchange:

  1. Introduction and Business Challenges
    Understand supply chain sustainability challenges and how SAP Sustainability Data Exchange enables collaboration between business partners.
  2. Leveraging Networks
    Learn how the solution integrates with networks like:
  • SAP Business Network
  • Catena-X
  1. Subscription and Setup
    Learn how to provision and adapt  SAP Sustainability Data Exchange within your SAP Business Technology Platform environment.
  2. Getting Started with Data Exchange

      Learn how to:

  • Maintain master data for products and business partners
  • Exchange product carbon footprint data
  • Understand end-to-end carbon management scenarios with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management

Who Should Take the Course?

This learning journey is ideal for:

  • Consultants supporting sustainability and SAP implementations

No prerequisites are required, making it a great starting point for beginners.

Start collaborating on supply chain sustainability today and explore the Learning Journey “Getting Started with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange” here.
